title: Scientists Found How Alzheimer's Actually Spreads
script: Alzheimer's spreads like a virus? For decades, scientists knew the disease crept from neuron to neuron — they just didn't know how a sick cell infects a healthy one. A new study out of the University of Utah just named the courier. It's a protein called Arc, and its normal job is harmless — until it starts packaging toxic tau, the tangled protein behind Alzheimer's, into tiny vesicles and shipping it straight into neighboring brain cells. One infected neuron becomes ten. Ten becomes a hundred. That's the spread doctors have watched for years without ever seeing the mechanism behind it. Block Arc, and you might block the delivery truck entirely — stopping the disease from spreading cell to cell in the first place. So what happens to treatment once you can shut the courier down?
